title: He Was Pushed Out at 63 in an AI Restructuring. Why This Couple Is Claiming His Wife’s Smaller Check First, Not His.
excerpt: When an AI restructuring wiped out his job at 63, this couple faced a Social Security timing decision that most spouses get completely backwards, and the sta...
content_html:
Gerelyn Terzo

Tue, July 21, 2026 at 4:02 AM MDT 5 min read

Quick Read

  • Claiming the wife's smaller benefit at 62 generates immediate cash flow while the husband's larger benefit grows 8% annually until age 70.

  • When one spouse dies, the survivor keeps only the larger benefit, making the husband's delayed check a permanent income floor for his wife.

  • With U.S. household debt at a record $18.8 trillion and a 3.9% savings rate, her early check bridges the gap without draining retirement accounts.

  • Are you ahead, or behind on retirement? SmartAsset's free tool can match you with a financial advisor in minutes to help you answer that today. Each advisor has been carefully vetted, and must act in your best interests. Don't waste another minute; learn more here.

Picture a couple in their early 60s. He spent decades as a mid-level manager, she worked longer stretches at lower pay while raising kids. At 63, his employer announces an AI-driven restructuring and his role disappears. The severance is decent but finite. Her paycheck is smaller and steady. They have some savings, a mortgage nearly paid off, and worry about bridging the next few years without tapping retirement accounts.

Stories like theirs are showing up more often in online retirement forums, where a spouse asks whether it is time to turn on Social Security. It is a fair question, especially when the June 2026 unemployment rate of 4.2% hides how choppy the job market has become for workers over 60.

Why the Smaller Check Goes First

The instinct most couples have is to turn on the bigger benefit because it produces more cash flow. In this situation, that is exactly backwards. The move that better protects them for the next 30 years is claiming the wife's smaller benefit early, while the husband's larger benefit keeps growing untouched until age 70.

_________________________________

What's Your Number...?

Here's a question most people 5y from retirement can't answer: at your current savings rate, how much do you need, and how long will it actually last? A good advisor can put a date on that in a single meeting. SmartAsset's free quiz matches you with up to three fiduciary advisors serving your area, so you can get YOUR retirement number now (sponsor)

__________________________________________

Two Social Security mechanics drive this. First, claiming before full retirement age (FRA) permanently lowers a benefit, with up to a 30% reduction if you claim at age 62. Second, delaying past FRA adds roughly 8% per year up to age 70. Those delayed credits are locked in for life and carried forward into the survivor benefit.

content_html_zh:
格林三世

MDT 2026 年 7 月 21 日星期二凌晨 4:02 阅读 5 分钟

快速阅读

  • 妻子在 62 岁时领取较小的福利，会立即产生现金流，而丈夫较大的福利则每年增长 8%，直到 70 岁。
  • 当其中一位配偶去世时，幸存者只保留较大的福利，这使得丈夫的延迟支票成为妻子的永久收入底线。
  • 由于美国家庭债务达到创纪录的 18.8 万亿美元，储蓄率为 3.9%，她的提前支票弥补了这一缺口，同时又不会耗尽退休账户的资金。
  • 你的退休年龄是领先还是落后？ SmartAsset 的免费工具可以在几分钟内为您匹配财务顾问，帮助您立即回答这个问题。 每位顾问都经过仔细审查，并且必须以您的最佳利益为出发点。 不要再浪费一分钟了；在这里了解更多信息。

想象一下一对 60 岁出头的夫妇。 他担任了几十年的中层经理，而她在抚养孩子的同时，以较低的工资工作了更长的时间。 63 岁时，他的雇主宣布进行人工智能驱动的重组，他的角色消失了。 遣散费虽然不错，但有限。 她的薪水较少但稳定。 他们有一些积蓄，抵押贷款几乎还清，并且担心在不动用退休账户的情况下度过未来几年的过渡期。

像他们这样的故事越来越多地出现在在线退休论坛上，配偶询问是否是时候开启社会保障了。 这是一个公平的问题，尤其是当 2026 年 6 月 4.2% 的失业率掩盖了 60 岁以上工人的就业市场有多么动荡时。

为什么较小的支票先行

大多数夫妇的本能是寻求更大的福利，因为它会产生更多的现金流。 在这种情况下，那就完全是倒退了。 在接下来的30年里，更好地保护他们的举措是尽早领取妻子较小的福利，而丈夫较大的福利则保持不变，直到70岁。

_________________________________

你的电话号码是多少...？

这是大多数退休后 5 岁的人无法回答的问题：按照您目前的储蓄率，您需要多少资金，以及实际能维持多久？ 一个好的顾问可以在一次会议上确定日期。 SmartAsset 的免费测验为您匹配最多三名为您所在地区服务的信托顾问，这样您现在就可以获得您的退休号码（赞助商）

__________________________________________

两个社会保障机制推动了这一点。 首先，在完全退休年龄 (FRA) 之前领取福利会永久降低福利，如果您在 62 岁时领取福利，最多可减少 30%。 其次，延迟通过 FRA 每年会增加约 8%，直到 70 岁。 这些延迟的积分将终身锁定，并转入幸存者福利。
